items.text: He had the reputation of being a very humane and Christian man, yet thought nothing of selling my poor old father, and dear aged mother.
items.context: William Craft describing the hypocrisy of his enslaver who claimed to be morally upright while engaging in the cruelty of separating families.
items.publicReaction: This contradiction between claimed morality and enslaving actions was widely cited by abolitionists to expose slavery's corrupting effect on supposedly 'Christian' slaveholders.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)

items.text: I...felt that the straps that bound the heavy burden to my back began to pop, and the load to roll off.
items.context: William Craft's vivid description of the emotional and physical relief he experienced when they arrived in Philadelphia and reached free soil.
items.publicReaction: This poetic passage was cited in antislavery literature as a powerful expression of the liberation felt upon escaping slavery.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)

items.text: One night…Lewis Hayden and I had a keg of gunpowder under his house in Phillips Street, with a fuse attached ready to light it should any attempt be made to capture us.
items.context: William Craft describing the desperate measures he and fellow abolitionist Lewis Hayden took in Boston to prepare armed resistance against slave catchers attempting to capture them under the Fugitive Slave Act.
items.publicReaction: This statement shocked and impressed readers with the Crafts' determination to defend their freedom by any means necessary, highlighting the urgency of their situation.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)

items.text: We were free from the horrible trammels of slavery, and glorifying God who had brought us safely out of a land of bondage.
items.context: William Craft's reflection on their successful escape and their gratitude for divine assistance in achieving freedom.
items.publicReaction: This statement combined the emotional and spiritual dimensions of their escape, resonating with both religious and secular antislavery audiences.
items.source: William Craft, Running a Thousand Miles for Freedom; or, The Escape of William and Ellen Craft from Slavery (London: William Tweedie, 1860)
